title: `cargo init` fails with "failed to resolve path '/home/user/myproject/.git/': Operation not permitted; class=Os (2)" on rootless archlinux container
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

I'm running rootless podman + non-root user with archlinux image. `cargo init` fails with `failed to resolve path '/home/user/myproject/.git/': Operation not permitted; class=Os (2)`.

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. For this Dockerfile
```Dockerfile
FROM archlinux

ARG uid
ARG gid
ARG user
ARG group

RUN pacman -Sy --noconfirm git rust \
	&& bash -c "echo yes | pacman -Scc" \
	&& groupadd -g ${gid} ${group} \
	&& useradd \
	-m \
	-g ${group} \
	--uid ${uid} \
	${user}

USER ${user}

WORKDIR /home/user
```

2. `$ podman build -t test_arch --build-arg uid=1000 --build-arg gid=1000 --build-arg user=user --build-arg group=user .`

3. `$ podman run --rm test_arch cargo init myproject`


**Describe the results you received:**
```
error: Failed to create package `myproject` at `/home/user/myproject`

Caused by:
  failed to resolve path '/home/user/myproject/.git/': Operation not permitted; class=Os (2)
```

**Describe the results you expected:**
```
     Created binary (application) package
```


**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**
On podman the container only works with `--privileged` or `--security-opt seccomp=unconfined` flags. It also works on rootless docker. An equivalent fedora image also works on podman. Tried to generate the seccomp profile to maybe see what it needs (followed instructions from https://podman.io/blogs/2019/10/15/generate-seccomp-profiles.html) but couldn't get it to create the file. 

The issue seems to come from git, if `git-fetch-with-cli` is enabled in cargo, it works on podman (https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/6652#issuecomment-530208629). It must be some syscalls that cargo makes when using its builtin git library which podman blocks?

It looks like recently there have been many issues due to some glibc changes (https://gitlab.archlinux.org/archlinux/archlinux-docker/-/issues/56) but podman's default seccomp should be up to date with that so I'm not sure where to pinpoint this issue as the container works with rootless docker out of the box.

Tried to debug with strace but couldn't quite tell the difference between failing and succeeding runs - the number of "Operation not permitted" errors were equal. If more debugging output is needed, I'll gladly post. 

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      3.1.0-dev
API Version:  3.1.0-dev
Go Version:   go1.16
Git Commit:   a9fcd9d7602ce59a704884c6840e27666fea20d1-dirty
Built:        Sat Mar  6 23:50:25 2021
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```

  conmon:

      host_id: 100000
      size: 65536
  kernel: 5.11.2-zen1-1-zen
  linkmode: dynamic
  memFree: 6469230592
  memTotal: 16674353152
  ociRuntime:
    name: crun
    package: /usr/bin/crun is owned by crun 0.18-1
    path: /usr/bin/crun
    version: |-
      crun version 0.18
      commit: 808420efe3dc2b44d6db9f1a3fac8361dde42a95

      +SYSTEMD +SELINUX +APPARMOR +CAP +SECCOMP +EBPF +YAJL
  os: linux
  remoteSocket:
    path: /run/user/1000/podman/podman.sock
  security:
    apparmorEnabled: false
    capabilities: CAP_CHOWN,CAP_DAC_OVERRIDE,CAP_FOWNER,CAP_FSETID,CAP_KILL,CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE,CAP_SETFCAP,CAP_SETGID,CAP_SETPCAP,CAP_SETUID,CAP_SYS_CHROOT
    rootless: true

  slirp4netns:

  swapFree: 0
```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

Installed from source.

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide?**

Yes

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

Bare metal host running latest Arch Linux.

comments.body:
I found the culprit:
```
[pid 229163] faccessat2(AT_FDCWD, "/home/user/testcargo/.git/", F_OK, AT_EACCESS <unfinished ...>
[pid 229157] read(8,  <unfinished ...>
[pid 229163] <... faccessat2 resumed>)  = -1 EPERM (Operation not permitted)
```

But:
```
> grep faccessat /usr/share/containers/seccomp.json
                                "faccessat",
                                "faccessat2",
```

When I specify `/usr/share/containers/seccomp.json` as the profile, it runs:
```
> podman run --rm --security-opt seccomp=/usr/share/containers/seccomp.json test_arch cargo init my
project
     Created binary (application) package
```

It turns out that podman by default loads the `/etc/containers/seccomp.json` file which is missing `faccessat2`:
```
> grep faccessat /etc/containers/seccomp.json
                                "faccessat",
```

On further inspection:
```
> pacman -Qo /etc/containers/seccomp.json
/etc/containers/seccomp.json is owned by containers-common 0.35.2-1
```
```
> pacman -Qo /usr/share/containers/seccomp.json
/usr/share/containers/seccomp.json is owned by containers-common 0.35.2-1
```

Why are there 2 different versions of the same file? Is this an upstream or a packaging issue?
Found out this was totally my blunder, pacman creates .pacnew files when configuration changes and I hadn't updated the file. But it's still misleading to have now two identical configuration files.
